what: what does weak mean in weakmap
why: why use weakmap instead of map
how: how does garbage collection work with weakmap
output: invalid value used as weak map key

1_beginner_explanation.summary: A **WeakMap** is exactly like a `Map`, but with two strange rules: 1) The keys MUST be objects (no strings/numbers allowed), and 2) The map holds those keys 'weakly'. This means if your application deletes all other references to that object, the WeakMap will automatically delete the object from itself to free up memory. It prevents Memory Leaks.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: A normal `Map` is like a hoarder. Even if everyone else throws away a piece of trash, the Map holds onto it forever. A `WeakMap` is a minimalist. If it notices that nobody else in the entire house cares about an item anymore, the WeakMap automatically throws it in the garbage.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: Because items can be silently deleted by the Garbage Collector at any unpredictable millisecond, WeakMaps cannot be looped over. There is no `weakmap.keys()` or `weakmap.size`. You can only `.set()`, `.get()`, and `.has()`.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: WeakMaps do not prevent Garbage Collection of their keys. If a DOM element is removed from the screen and set to null, a WeakMap holding that element as a key will instantly drop it, recovering Heap memory.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Tracking data about DOM elements without causing memory leaks when those elements are removed from the page.

4_common_confusion.trap: Trying to use a primitive value (like a string or number) as a key in a WeakMap. It will throw a TypeError.
4_common_confusion.fix: WeakMaps ONLY accept Objects as keys, because primitives are not garbage collected in the same way.

6_edge_case.quirk: Before `#privateFields` existed, developers used WeakMaps to store truly private data for classes, using the class instance `this` as the WeakMap key.
